      Common owners and mergers
   =20
 =20
Here=E2=80=99s a=C2=A0simple good=C2=A0model of public-company mergers and =
acquisitions:=09An acquirer should try to acquire a target if the target is=
 worth more to the acquirer than its stock market value. Because of synergi=
es (cost savings or revenue opportunities from combining the companies), or=
 because the market undervalues the target, or because it has clever strate=
gic plans or=C2=A0whatever.=09A target should agree to be acquired if the p=
rice the acquirer offers is higher than its realistic standalone value. Mea=
ning higher than its stock market value today, but also higher=C2=A0than it=
 could get from another acquirer or from management=E2=80=99s cool new long=
-term plan.=09If the target really is worth more to the acquirer than its s=
tandalone value, they should negotiate to split that value among themselves=
: The acquirer should pay some of it to the target=E2=80=99s shareholders a=
s a premium to the market price, but not all of it, because the acquirer=E2=
=80=99s shareholders should get some upside from the deal too.In practice a=
cquirers sometimes make acquisitions for bad, non-economic reasons. The chi=
ef executive officer of the acquirer thinks it would be fun to run a bigger=
 company (because it makes her feel more important, or because it will allo=
w her to demand higher pay), so she buys up other companies to make hers bi=
gger. And a target may accept or reject an offer=C2=A0for bad reasons: Its =
CEO is tired of running the company and wants to cash out with a big golden=
 parachute, so she takes a bad offer, or its CEO likes running a company (a=
nd feeling important and getting a paycheck), so she refuses an offer that =
is better than the company=E2=80=99s standalone prospects.In theory the sha=
reholders can limit deals made for=C2=A0bad reasons. The target=E2=80=99s s=
hareholders generally have to agree to any deal; if the target CEO accepts =
a bad deal the shareholders can vote it down. If the target CEO rejects a g=
ood deal, the acquirer can generally =E2=80=9Cgo hostile,=E2=80=9D taking t=
he deal directly to the target=E2=80=99s shareholders, and they can overrul=
e the CEO. The=C2=A0acquirer=E2=80=99s shareholders often don=E2=80=99t get=
 to vote on the deal=C2=A0(depending on how big it is and how it is=C2=A0st=
ructured), but even if they don=E2=80=99t specifically get a vote on the de=
al they can express their displeasure, run a proxy fight to vote out bad ma=
nagers, etc.And so you might expect the shareholders to enforce the simple =
good model. Target shareholders will want deals that are worth more, to the=
m, than the standalone target; acquirer shareholders will want deals that i=
ncrease the value of the acquirer.Except that those shareholders are the sa=
me shareholders. The dominant shareholders in modern public companies are u=
niversal shareholders, giant asset managers who own big stakes in all the c=
ompanies. This messes up that model. If you own the acquirer and the target=
 in roughly equal proportions, you might not care very much if the deal is =
good or the premium is fair. If you own a lot of the acquirer but even more=
 of the target, you might want the acquirer to make a bad deal and overpay,=
 because you have a bigger share in being overpaid than in overpaying.More =
generally, if you own all of the companies, you might favor mergers that re=
duce competition. If the acquirer buys the target and reduces competition i=
n the industry, you will benefit as (1) a shareholder of the target (you ge=
t cashed out at a premium), (2) a shareholder of the acquirer (you now own =
a bigger company in a less competitive industry which can charge higher pri=
ces), and (3) a shareholder of the=C2=A0other=C2=A0companies=C2=A0in the in=
dustry, who also now face less competition.For that matter, you might favor=
 mergers that are stupid. If the acquirer makes a dumb acquisition and over=
pays for it, you will lose out, as a shareholder of the acquirer, but you w=
ill benefit as (1) a shareholder of the target (you get cashed out at a big=
 premium) and (2) a shareholder of the other companies in the industry, whi=
ch=C2=A0now face less competition from the target (which has been acquired)=
 and from the acquirer (which has made a stupid acquisition and will now be=
 distracted by having to deal with it).=C2=A0Here is a new paper called =E2=
=80=9CBeyond the target: M&A decisions and rival ownership,=E2=80=9D by Mig=
uel Ant=C3=B3n, Jos=C3=A9 Azar, Mireia Gine and Luca Lin in the Journal of =
Financial Economics:Diversified acquirer shareholders can profit from value=
-destroying acquisitions not only through their target stakes, but also thr=
ough stakes in non-merging rival firms. Announcement losses are largely mit=
igated for the average acquirer shareholder when accounting for wealth effe=
cts on their rival stakes. Ownership by acquirer shareholders in non-mergin=
g rivals is negatively associated with deal quality and positively associat=
ed with deal completion. Funds with more rival ownership are more likely to=
 vote in favor of the acquisition. Overall, these results show that many so=
-called =E2=80=9Cbad deals=E2=80=9D are often in the interest of acquirer-f=
irm shareholders.Here=E2=80=99s a simple example from the paper:Consider th=
e following example: when Microsoft announced the $26.2 billion acquisition=
 of LinkedIn in June 2016, the deal was perceived as value-destroying by th=
e market and led to a loss of 1.46% for Microsoft shareholders in the 3-day=
 window around the announcement. With a market capitalization of over $400 =
billion at the time, the losses for Microsoft=E2=80=99s largest shareholder=
s were substantial, ranging from $72 million to $373 million, as shown in F=
ig. 1. Nine of these top ten shareholders also owned shares in the target. =
While LinkedIn did enjoy a large announcement gain of 45.97%, only two acqu=
irer shareholders were able to offset its loss on Microsoft with a gain fro=
m LinkedIn.However =E2=80=93and this is the main point of this paper=E2=80=
=93 nine of Microsoft=E2=80=99s top ten institutional shareholders obtained=
 a net gain thanks to the wealth effects from their ownership in rival firm=
s. This was because, among Microsoft=E2=80=99s top twenty industry rivals, =
fifteen gained during the 3-day window around this announcement, and these =
gains were more than enough to compensate these shareholders for their loss=
es from their holdings in Microsoft. Therefore, even though the deal may ha=
ve seemed to be value-destroying, it actually created value for most of the=
 top 10 shareholders of Microsoft.There are obvious reasons to study this e=
ffect in mergers. Mergers are big events in corporate life, shareholders pa=
y attention to them and often get to vote on them, they attract a lot of ac=
ademic attention, and there is longstanding academic suspicion that lots of=
 mergers are bad and destroy value, so explaining how that happens =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0why shareholders let managers get away with bad mergers =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0is useful.But if you believe this mechanism there=E2=80=99s no reason=
 to think it applies=C2=A0only=C2=A0to mergers.=C2=A0Any=C2=A0stupid busine=
ss decision can be good for a company's competitors. In general common owne=
rs will prefer good decisions to bad ones (because good decisions tend to b=
e positive-sum and common shareholders want to grow overall corporate profi=
ts), but you could imagine situations where they might prefer bad ones. For=
 instance, they might own more of the=C2=A0competitors than they do of the =
company doing the dumb thing. Or,=C2=A0because the upside in stocks is unli=
mited but the downside is floored at zero, a=C2=A0very=C2=A0dumb decision b=
y one company might be good for common shareholders.=C2=A0(If a $10 billion=
 company does something dumb that (1) costs it $50 billion and (2) makes=C2=
=A0its public=C2=A0competitors $30 billion richer, common shareholders get =
all $30 billion of the benefit but only $10 billion of the loss: The compan=
y goes bankrupt, the stock goes to zero, and the other $40 billion of losse=
s are someone else=E2=80=99s problem.[1]) Any time a corporate CEO meets wi=
th her diversified shareholders and they suggest a bold new business idea, =
she has to ponder: Do they think this is a good=C2=A0idea, or do they think=
 it=E2=80=99s a=C2=A0hilariously stupid=C2=A0idea that will benefit their=
=C2=A0other=C2=A0portfolio companies?Elsewhere, here is Adam Tooze on =E2=
=80=9Casset manager capitalism.=E2=80=9D Asset manager capitalism is kind o=
f weird, is my basic point here.

   =20
      Common owners and vaccines
   =20
 =20
It seems reasonable to say that pandemics are bad for the world economy and=
 that curing or preventing them would be good. If you are a big diversified=
 institutional shareholder who owns a big chunk of all the companies in the=
 world, ending the Covid-19 pandemic would be very good for your portfolio.=
 This was particularly true in the early days of the pandemic in 2020, when=
 much of the world economy was severely disrupted and vaccines were still i=
n development. Back then, I sometimes  argued that the best thing a big pub=
lic pharmaceutical company could do, for its shareholders, would be to (1) =
develop a Covid-19 vaccine or cure, (2) spend as much money as possible to=
=C2=A0manufacture it as quickly as possible and (3) give it away for free. =
This might be financially disastrous for that company, and its stock might =
go down, and its shareholders might lose money=C2=A0on that stock. But most=
 shareholders these days are broadly diversified; the shareholders own lots=
 of=C2=A0other=C2=A0stocks, which would go up. Making a vaccine widely avai=
lable would be hugely positive-sum for corporate profits, and thus corporat=
e shareholders, as a whole, even if it was costly for the particular compan=
y making the vaccine. And since that company=E2=80=99s shareholders =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0who,=C2=A0at least in some sense, own the company=C2=A0 =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0also own all the other companies, and understand all this, they might=
 pressure the company to sacrifice its own interest for the common good.Two=
 years later the evidence on that is, let us say, mixed; there were  some r=
eports of common shareholders pushing pharmaceutical companies to cooperate=
 to develop vaccines faster, but=C2=A0the vaccine makers have made huge pro=
fits and seem to have done well for themselves, while large parts=C2=A0of t=
he world are unvaccinated.=C2=A0You could imagine common shareholders being=
 disappointed by this. You could imagine them thinking that the world econo=
my and broad corporate profits are at risk as long as global vaccination ra=
tes are low, and that the best thing for their overall portfolios would be =
for the vaccine makers to try harder to distribute vaccines freely even at =
the cost of their own profits. You could imagine them asking the big vaccin=
e makers to do that.=C2=A0I suppose it is a little far-fetched but on the o=
ther hand it is happening! Kind of.=C2=A0Last November, a small shareholder=
 in Johnson & Johnson filed a shareholder proposal, for J&J=E2=80=99s proxy=
 statement this spring, asking the company to =E2=80=9Ccommission and publi=
sh a report on (1) the public health costs created by the limited sharing o=
f the Company=E2=80=99s COVID 19 vaccine technologies and any consequent re=
duced availability in poorer nations and (2) the manner in which such costs=
 may affect the market returns available to its diversified shareholders.=
=E2=80=9D[2] The proposal goes on:To the extent our Company is increasing i=
ts own financial returns by preventing vaccine production in poorer nations=
, its own increased profits are coming at a severe cost to the global econo=
my, because failure to vaccinate the world=E2=80=99s vulnerable communities=
 is inhibiting worldwide economic recovery and creating opportunities for m=
ore dangerous SARS CoV 2 variants to develop.This is a bad trade for most o=
f the Company=E2=80=99s shareholders, who are diversified and thus rely on =
broad economic growth to achieve their financial objectives. A Company stra=
tegy that increases its own financial returns but threatens global GDP is c=
ounter to the best interests of most of its shareholders: the potential dra=
g on GDP created by hoarding vaccine technology will directly reduce divers=
ified portfolio returns over the long term.Despite this risk, the Company h=
as not disclosed any analysis of the trade offs between Company profit and =
global public health from the perspective of its largely diversified shareh=
olders, whose investment portfolios may be at grave risk from undue limitat=
ions on vaccine production.The requested report will help shareholders dete=
rmine whether current Company policies serve shareholders=E2=80=99 best int=
erests.J&J asked the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission if it could om=
it this proposal from its proxy statement, and the SEC said no, so J&J=E2=
=80=99s=C2=A0shareholders will get to vote on it.I don't want to make too m=
uch of this. This is not J&J=E2=80=99s biggest shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
Vanguard Group, BlackRock Inc. and State Street Corp. =E2=80=94=C2=A0asking=
 the company to license its vaccines; this is a small shareholder making tr=
ouble with a nonbinding proposal asking J&J to produce a report. I just wan=
t to point out that this is a thing now; you can write a shareholder propos=
al asking a company=E2=80=99s shareholders to think about their interests a=
s diversified shareholders, and asking the company to prioritize those inte=
rests above its own economic performance.We  talked previously about a shar=
eholder proposal at Fox Corp., asking it to become a public benefit corpora=
tion focused on informing its viewers; that was, I think, the first case of=
 a shareholder proposal specifically arguing that it was in the best intere=
sts of Fox=E2=80=99s diversified shareholders to do something that was not =
in the company=E2=80=99s direct financial interest. That one got about 1% o=
f the vote at Fox=E2=80=99s annual meeting so, you know, early days, but th=
is stuff is part of the conversation now. The model of financial capitalism=
 for many decades was that a company=E2=80=99s shareholders were interested=
 exclusively in the financial performance of the company. Now new models ar=
e available.

      Affirm oopsie
   =20
 =20
One of my  favorite insider trading cases is the one of the hackers who all=
egedly got advance access to tens of thousands of corporate press releases =
and used the confidential information in those press releases to trade stoc=
ks. An SEC case against some of them alleged that they  made money on about=
 77% of their trades. That=E2=80=99s pretty good, but it also means that ro=
ughly one out of every four times that they=C2=A0got the news in advance=C2=
=A0they nonetheless misinterpreted it. A company would write an earnings re=
lease with a big top-line beat but disappointing net income, and the trader=
s would have to decide =E2=80=9Cwait is this good news or bad news?=E2=80=
=9D They got it wrong about one out of every four times.=C2=A0It would be h=
ilarious if companies regularly put out earnings releases one item at a tim=
e, an hour apart. =E2=80=9COur top-line revenue was up 77%,=E2=80=9D a comp=
any would say, and investors would be like =E2=80=9Csweet!=E2=80=9D and buy=
 a lot of stock, and then an hour later the company would say =E2=80=9Cbut =
we had a $160 million net loss,=E2=80=9D and investors would rush to sell. =
An hour later you=E2=80=99d get guidance for next quarter.=C2=A0Knowing tha=
t a company beat expectations on revenue is=C2=A0helpful, but it is not=C2=
=A0definitive. If you want to know what the stock will do, you need all=C2=
=A0of the earnings information, plus you need to know what the market was e=
xpecting. Even then it is not always easy.=C2=A0Anyway companies do not act=
ually do this because (1) it=E2=80=99s stupid and (2) it is=C2=A0against th=
e rules to =E2=80=9Comit to state a material fact necessary in order to mak=
e the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they w=
ere made, not misleading,=E2=80=9D meaning you can't just put out good news=
 without mentioning the related bad news. I mean, they don=E2=80=99t do it =
intentionally, for the most part. But Affirm  did it by accident last week:=
Affirm Holdings Inc. tumbled after the buy-now, pay-later firm whipsawed in=
vestors with an errant, early release of partial financial results and then=
 forecast quarterly revenue that missed analyst estimates.The company=E2=80=
=99s shares plunged as much as 33% on Thursday afternoon after it reported =
results with a third-quarter revenue forecast that missed some analyst esti=
mates, and a widened net loss. Just hours earlier, investors were given a s=
napshot of what the company called =E2=80=9Canother great quarter=E2=80=9D =
via an accidental Tweet, which was enough of a glimpse to send the shares s=
urging.The afternoon volatility caused a halt in trading -- and prompted th=
e company to explain itself -- again on Twitter. Some results had leaked ea=
rlier than the official earnings release time, scheduled for after the mark=
et closed, =E2=80=9Cdue to human error,=E2=80=9D it said.The second-quarter=
 results, which it went on to release ahead of schedule anyway, showed a 77=
% gain in revenue. Its net loss of $159.7 million widened from $26.6 millio=
n a year earlier, missing the $100.3 million average estimate of six analys=
ts in a Bloomberg survey.=C2=A0In the accidental Tweet posted at about 1:15=
 p.m., the company had referenced the revenue surge, before deleting the po=
st.=E2=80=9CEverything is securities fraud,=E2=80=9D I often say around her=
e, and this is something, so at least one law firm is already drumming up a=
 lawsuit over it.

      Sure whatever MoviePass
   =20
 =20
A funny=C2=A0business model for MoviePass would be:=09You pay $10 a month f=
or one MoviePass Token.=09You can use the MoviePass Token to see unlimited =
movies in theaters each month, with MoviePass buying the tickets for you at=
 full price.=09Or=C2=A0you can =E2=80=9Cstake=E2=80=9D the MoviePass Token,=
 locking it up in MoviePass=E2=80=99s treasury.=09Staked MoviePass Tokens c=
annot be used to buy movie tickets.=09But for each MoviePass Token that you=
 stake you get an additional, say, 0.3 MoviePass Tokens per month.=09After =
just a few months you=E2=80=99ll own, like, five MoviePass Tokens.=09This d=
oes not really help you see any more movies but five is definitely more tha=
n one.=09I don=E2=80=99t even know man.I just feel like one of the advantag=
es of pivoting to crypto is that instead of charging people money for a use=
ful product that is expensive to produce, you can charge them money for a p=
roduct that does not exist? =E2=80=9CYou pay $10 a month and instead of mov=
ie tickets we will give you non-fungible tokens of movie tickets, which are=
 like movie tickets except that (1) they are on the blockchain, (2) they ar=
e a tradable speculative asset and (3) you can=E2=80=99t use them to see mo=
vies.=E2=80=9DAnyway MoviePass=E2=80=99s actual web3 pivot is less interest=
ing:Two years after the wildly popular subscription service MoviePass crash=
ed and burned, its co-founder Stacy Spikes is determined to bring it back. =
Spikes says MoviePass will return this summer.The company, recently bought =
by Spikes after his unceremonious ouster from MoviePass in 2018, held its l=
aunch event today at the Walter Reade Theater at Lincoln Center in NYC. ...=
He hyped the relaunch under his new vision, which, he says, will be =E2=80=
=9Cpowered by web3 technology.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6=C2=A0Under the new model,=
 MoviePass will run on tradable credits that roll over month to month. Subs=
cribers will also be able to use their credits to bring a friend, a markedl=
y different approach from the single-user card system that MoviePass used p=
reviously, which could prove annoying for non-cardholders.MoviePass 2.0 wil=
l also work on a tiered system, Spikes said. Spikes shared images of a beta=
 version of the new app and the credit-based system, which will vary based =
on things like peak moviegoing hours. =E2=80=A6Somewhat unsurprisingly, Mov=
iePass will incorporate aspects of Spikes=E2=80=99 existing business PreSho=
w, a technology that has been used to allow gamers to trade ad views for in=
-game currency. (This sort of format for moviegoing was previously teased b=
y Spikes to Insider.) The way that PreShow works is that you have to actual=
ly view the ad =E2=80=94 it tracks whether your attention is trained on you=
r phone. Once users =E2=80=9Ccomplete the PreShow,=E2=80=9D they=E2=80=99ll=
 be awarded credits that can then be used to buy tickets through the MovieP=
ass platform.I feel like broadcast television figured out this model =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cyou can watch a movie for free, though not on opening wee=
kend, if you also watch some ads=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0many decades ago? =
Imagine going to someone sitting in front of their television in like 1985,=
 watching =E2=80=9CFletch=E2=80=9D on CBS, and saying =E2=80=9Cin the futur=
e you will also be able to watch movies with ads, but your screen will watc=
h your eyeballs to make sure you're paying attention to the ads.=E2=80=9D W=
hy is that better.

   =20
      Elsewhere in staking
   =20
 =20
Here=E2=80=99s a Bloomberg story about  staking in crypto:Staking has been =
booming in part because of the incentive-based aspect of crypto where vario=
us new coins and blockchains are competing for validators by promising stra=
tospheric annual returns in the form of new coins. The rewards have been so=
 lucrative that more than 70% of all tokens issued on many chains -- Solana=
, Binance Smart Chain and Cardano, among them -- were staked late last year=
, according to crypto researcher Messari and tracker Staking Rewards.As sta=
king options multiply and promised returns reach into the triple digits, th=
e trend has only strengthened. In the fourth quarter, 7.7% of all the coins=
 that make up the roughly $2 trillion crypto universe were staked, up from =
1.8% in the year-ago period, according to staking provider Staked, a unit o=
f the crypto exchange Kraken. ...Still, many sophisticated crypto investors=
 who are holding their crypto for the long term are pouring their funds int=
o staking to earn yields -- and to beat crypto inflation. In proof-of-stake=
 blockchains, stashes of coins help the networks order transactions, and th=
ese stashes earn new coins the network generates in return. Those who don=
=E2=80=99t stake are losing out on this new coin issuance, akin to inflatio=
n.I do love the economics of this pitch: =E2=80=9CYou should buy Zorblocks,=
 because if you hold a Zorblock for a week it will become two Zorblocks, an=
d within a month you=E2=80=99ll have sixteen Zorblocks, which makes your or=
iginal Zorblock a good investment.=E2=80=9D Much of crypto economics consis=
ts of  some version of =E2=80=9Cif you assume this thing is valuable, then =
it is valuable.=E2=80=9D That is true in some loose sense of lots of other =
investments, too, but crypto has really managed it at scale.

<td class=3D"body-component__content" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; m=

10px;">
<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Common owners and mergers</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here=E2=80=99s a=C2=A0simple good=C2=
=A0model of public-company mergers and acquisitions:</p><ol style=3D"list-s=
tyle-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"ma=
rgin-bottom: 5px;">An acquirer should try to acquire a target if the target=
 is worth more to the acquirer than its stock market value. Because of syne=
rgies (cost savings or revenue opportunities from combining the companies),=
 or because the market undervalues the target, or because it has clever str=
ategic plans or=C2=A0whatever.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">A t=
arget should agree to be acquired if the price the acquirer offers is highe=
r than its realistic standalone value. Meaning higher than its stock market=
 value today, but also higher=C2=A0than it could get from another acquirer =
or from management=E2=80=99s cool new long-term plan.</li>=09<li style=3D"m=
argin-bottom: 5px;">If the target really is worth more to the acquirer than=
 its standalone value, they should negotiate to split that value among them=
selves: The acquirer should pay some of it to the target=E2=80=99s sharehol=
ders as a premium to the market price, but not all of it, because the acqui=
rer=E2=80=99s shareholders should get some upside from the deal too.</li></=
ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In practice acquirers sometimes make acquis=
itions for bad, non-economic reasons. The chief executive officer of the ac=
quirer thinks it would be fun to run a bigger company (because it makes her=
 feel more important, or because it will allow her to demand higher pay), s=
o she buys up other companies to make hers bigger. And a target may accept =
or reject an offer=C2=A0for bad reasons: Its CEO is tired of running the co=
mpany and wants to cash out with a big golden parachute, so she takes a bad=
 offer, or its CEO likes running a company (and feeling important and getti=
ng a paycheck), so she refuses an offer that is better than the company=E2=
=80=99s standalone prospects.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In theory the=
 shareholders can limit deals made for=C2=A0bad reasons. The target=E2=80=
=99s shareholders generally have to agree to any deal; if the target CEO ac=
cepts a bad deal the shareholders can vote it down. If the target CEO rejec=
ts a good deal, the acquirer can generally =E2=80=9Cgo hostile,=E2=80=9D ta=
king the deal directly to the target=E2=80=99s shareholders, and they can o=
verrule the CEO. The=C2=A0acquirer=E2=80=99s shareholders often don=E2=80=
=99t get to vote on the deal=C2=A0(depending on how big it is and how it is=
=C2=A0structured), but even if they don=E2=80=99t specifically get a vote o=
n the deal they can express their displeasure, run a proxy fight to vote ou=
t bad managers, etc.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so you might expec=
t the shareholders to enforce the simple good model. Target shareholders wi=
ll want deals that are worth more, to them, than the standalone target; acq=
uirer shareholders will want deals that increase the value of the acquirer.=
</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Except that those shareholders are the sam=
e shareholders. The dominant shareholders in modern public companies are un=
iversal shareholders, giant asset managers who own big stakes in all the co=
mpanies. This messes up that model. If you own the acquirer and the target =
in roughly equal proportions, you might not care very much if the deal is g=
ood or the premium is fair. If you own a lot of the acquirer but even more =
of the target, you might want the acquirer to make a bad deal and overpay, =
because you have a bigger share in being overpaid than in overpaying.</p><p=
 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">More generally, if you own all of the companies,=
 you might favor mergers that reduce competition. If the acquirer buys the =
target and reduces competition in the industry, you will benefit as (1) a s=
hareholder of the target (you get cashed out at a premium), (2) a sharehold=
er of the acquirer (you now own a bigger company in a less competitive indu=
stry which can charge higher prices), and (3) a shareholder of the=C2=A0<em=
>other=C2=A0</em>companies=C2=A0in the industry, who also now face less com=
petition.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For that matter, you might favor =
mergers that are stupid. If the acquirer makes a dumb acquisition and overp=
ays for it, you will lose out, as a shareholder of the acquirer, but you wi=
ll benefit as (1) a shareholder of the target (you get cashed out at a big =
premium) and (2) a shareholder of the other companies in the industry, whic=
h=C2=A0now face less competition from the target (which has been acquired) =
and from the acquirer (which has made a stupid acquisition and will now be =
distracted by having to deal with it).=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">Here is a new paper called =E2=80=9C<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/26707919.284607/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2NpZW5jZWRpcmVjdC5jb20=
vc2NpZW5jZS9hcnRpY2xlL2Ficy9waWkvUzAzMDQ0MDVYMjIwMDAwNTg/607f07ceef4b8524a3=
19a23cB575273b3" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_=
blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; =
background-color: #ccc;">Beyond the target: M&A decisions and rival own=
ership</a>,=E2=80=9D by Miguel Ant=C3=B3n, Jos=C3=A9 Azar, Mireia Gine and =
Luca Lin in the Journal of Financial Economics:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"=
margin: 16px 0;">Diversified acquirer shareholders can profit from value-de=
stroying acquisitions not only through their target stakes, but also throug=
h stakes in non-merging rival firms. Announcement losses are largely mitiga=
ted for the average acquirer shareholder when accounting for wealth effects=
 on their rival stakes. Ownership by acquirer shareholders in non-merging r=
ivals is negatively associated with deal quality and positively associated =
with deal completion. Funds with more rival ownership are more likely to vo=
te in favor of the acquisition. Overall, these results show that many so-ca=
lled =E2=80=9Cbad deals=E2=80=9D are often in the interest of acquirer-firm=
 shareholders.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here=E2=80=99s =
a simple example from the paper:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">Consider the following example: when Microsoft announced the $26.2 billio=
n acquisition of LinkedIn in June 2016, the deal was perceived as value-des=
troying by the market and led to a loss of 1.46% for Microsoft shareholders=
 in the 3-day window around the announcement. With a market capitalization =
of over $400 billion at the time, the losses for Microsoft=E2=80=99s larges=
t shareholders were substantial, ranging from $72 million to $373 million, =
as shown in Fig. 1. Nine of these top ten shareholders also owned shares in=
 the target. While LinkedIn did enjoy a large announcement gain of 45.97%, =
only two acquirer shareholders were able to offset its loss on Microsoft wi=
th a gain from LinkedIn.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">However =E2=80=93a=
nd this is the main point of this paper=E2=80=93 nine of Microsoft=E2=80=99=
s top ten institutional shareholders obtained a net gain thanks to the weal=
th effects from their ownership in rival firms. This was because, among Mic=
rosoft=E2=80=99s top twenty industry rivals, fifteen gained during the 3-da=
y window around this announcement, and these gains were more than enough to=
 compensate these shareholders for their losses from their holdings in Micr=
osoft. Therefore, even though the deal may have seemed to be value-destroyi=
ng, it actually created value for most of the top 10 shareholders of Micros=
oft.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are obvious reasons=
 to study this effect in mergers. Mergers are big events in corporate life,=
 shareholders pay attention to them and often get to vote on them, they att=
ract a lot of academic attention, and there is longstanding academic suspic=
ion that lots of mergers are bad and destroy value, so explaining how that =
happens =E2=80=94=C2=A0why shareholders let managers get away with bad merg=
ers =E2=80=94=C2=A0is useful.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But if you be=
lieve this mechanism there=E2=80=99s no reason to think it applies=C2=A0<em=
>only=C2=A0</em>to mergers.=C2=A0<em>Any=C2=A0</em>stupid business decision=
 can be good for a company's competitors. In general common owners will pre=
fer good decisions to bad ones (because good decisions tend to be positive-=
sum and common shareholders want to grow overall corporate profits), but yo=
u could imagine situations where they might prefer bad ones. For instance, =
they might own more of the=C2=A0competitors than they do of the company doi=
ng the dumb thing. Or,=C2=A0because the upside in stocks is unlimited but t=
he downside is floored at zero, a=C2=A0<em>very=C2=A0</em>dumb decision by =
one company might be good for common shareholders.=C2=A0(If a $10 billion c=
ompany does something dumb that (1) costs it $50 billion and (2) makes=C2=
=A0its public=C2=A0competitors $30 billion richer, common shareholders get =
all $30 billion of the benefit but only $10 billion of the loss: The compan=
y goes bankrupt, the stock goes to zero, and the other $40 billion of losse=
s are someone else=E2=80=99s problem.[1]) Any time a corporate CEO meets wi=
th her diversified shareholders and they suggest a bold new business idea, =
she has to ponder: Do they think this is a <em>good=C2=A0</em>idea, or do t=
hey think it=E2=80=99s a=C2=A0<em>hilariously stupid=C2=A0</em>idea that wi=
ll benefit their=C2=A0<em>other=C2=A0</em>portfolio companies?</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Elsewhere, here is Adam Tooze on =E2=80=9C<a href=3D"h=
ttps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26707919.284607/aHR0cHM6Ly9hZG=
FtdG9vemUuc3Vic3RhY2suY29tL3AvY2hhcnRib29rLTgyLXRoZS1yaXNlLW9mLWFzc2V0LW1hb=
mFnZXI/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB04ec158b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">asset manager capitalism=
</a>.=E2=80=9D Asset manager capitalism is kind of weird, is my basic point=
 here.</p><table class=3D"liveintent-sponsored-content" width=3D"auto" cell=

, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Common owners and vaccines</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It seems reasonable to say that pandem=
ics are bad for the world economy and that curing or preventing them would =
be good. If you are a big diversified institutional shareholder who owns a =
big chunk of all the companies in the world, ending the Covid-19 pandemic w=
ould be very good for your portfolio. This was particularly true in the ear=
ly days of the pandemic in 2020, when much of the world economy was severel=
y disrupted and vaccines were still in development. Back then, I <a href=3D=
"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26707919.284607/aHR0cHM6Ly93=
d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL25ld3NsZXR0ZXJzLzIwMjAtMDMtMTkvbW9uZXktc3R1ZmY=
tY3VyaW5nLWEtcGFuZGVtaWMtY291bGQtbWFrZS15b3UtcmljaD9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMjE0MjJfTU=
9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVyb=
T0yMjAyMTQmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBdd0b305=
d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">sometimes</a> <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.c=
om/click/26707919.284607/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydG=
ljbGVzLzIwMjAtMDctMjMveW91LWRvbi10LW5lZWQtcHJvZml0cy1hbnltb3JlP2NtcGlkPUJCR=
DAyMTQyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVy=
JnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDIxNCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB28735768" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bl=
ank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;"> argued</a> that the best thing a big public pharmac=
eutical company could do, for its shareholders, would be to (1) develop a C=
ovid-19 vaccine or cure, (2) spend as much money as possible to=C2=A0manufa=
cture it as quickly as possible and (3) give it away for free. This might b=
e financially disastrous for that company, and its stock might go down, and=
 its shareholders might lose money=C2=A0<em>on that stock</em>. But most sh=
areholders these days are broadly diversified; the shareholders own lots of=
=C2=A0<em>other=C2=A0</em>stocks, which would go up. Making a vaccine widel=
y available would be hugely positive-sum for corporate profits, and thus co=
rporate shareholders, as a whole, even if it was costly for the particular =
company making the vaccine. And since that company=E2=80=99s shareholders =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0who,=C2=A0at least in some sense, own the company=C2=A0 =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0also own all the other companies, and understand all this, they=
 might pressure the company to sacrifice its own interest for the common go=
od.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Two years later the evidence on that is=
, let us say, mixed; there were <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusin=
ess.com/click/26707919.284607/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL=

r Messari and tracker Staking Rewards.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As s=

enerates in return. Those who don=E2=80=99t stake are losing out on this ne=
w coin issuance, akin to inflation.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16p=
